skillName: Mental strategies for multiplication and division
misconceptionSummary: Students think halving one number and doubling the other changes the product.

visuals.ruleText: Doubling one factor and halving the other keeps the product the same
visuals.subText: a × b = (2a) × (b/2) — you multiply by 2 and divide by 2, net effect zero

steps.narration: Doubling one number multiplies the product by two. Halving the other divides it by two. The two effects cancel — the product is unchanged. This is a guaranteed equivalence, not a trick.

lines.text: Halved width, doubled height — area unchanged

steps.narration: Think of it as reshaping a rectangle. An eighteen by five rectangle has area ninety. Halve the width to nine and double the height to ten — it is a nine by ten rectangle, still area ninety.
Chained doubling works the same way. Thirteen times eight — double thirteen three times: twenty-six, fifty-two, one hundred and four. Each doubling is trivial; the chain gives the answer.
One hundred and four. Three doublings of thirteen. The product of any number and a power of two can be found by repeated doubling alone.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking 24 × 5 ≠ 12 × 10 because 'the numbers changed'. The product is invariant — halving one factor and doubling the other always preserves the result.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Halving and doubling cancel each other. The product stays the same because you multiplied by two and divided by two — a net change of zero.
